title: BATTLE OF THE ROCKETS 2026
what: Designed, analyzed, and launched two high-power rockets for the 2026 Battle of the Rockets competition (FGE): a Mars Rover delivery vehicle (79.125 in, 19.30 lb, AeroTech J540R-14, K-class, target apogee 1,000–2,000 ft) and a Target Altitude rocket (42.75 in, 1.924 lb, F42-8T, target 801 ft using tube fins only) — both compliant with FGE, NAR, and FAA safety standards and reviewed through PDR and CDR milestones

Led aerodynamic design through multi-physics simulation, iterative fin configuration optimization (trapezoidal and tube geometries), and full vehicle CAD integration in SolidWorks; both vehicles required ≥ 5:1 thrust-to-weight ratio and full recoverability

Contributed CFD analysis (unsteady URANS in OpenFOAM) to quantify instantaneous drag under accelerating subsonic flow — decomposing pressure vs. viscous contributions and validating design choices under non-equilibrium conditions
how: Aerodynamic design: iterated trapezoidal (4→3 PETG-CF fins) and tube fin (3→6, doubled mid-project for static margin recovery) geometries in SolidWorks; ran RockSim stability sweeps targeting stability 2.0–2.5 cal; final simulated T/W ratios 11.03:1 (Altitude) and 6.85:1 (Mars Rover)

CFD validation: built custom OpenFOAM URANS pipeline (snappyHexMesh, k-ω SST turbulence, time-dependent inlet BC); post-processed drag evolution in ParaView to inform nose cone optimization and validate pressure vs. viscous drag decomposition

Fabrication: transitioned from Blue Tube to phenolic cardboard after warping failure (reduced weight and assembly time); epoxied centering rings and tube fins, added Plastalina clay deadweight aft to optimize CG; reduced Altitude parachute 36→24 in for safe descent; managed BOM/budget and coordinated across recovery and payload subteams
results: Mars Rover: 1,459 ft apogee (within 1,000–2,000 ft spec) — Mach 0.2, 4.22 G peak boost, 12 ft/s descent, 129.7 s total flight; successful rover deployment and recovery
Altitude Rocket: 758 ft apogee — 43 ft from 801 ft target (<6% error), Mach 0.2, 3.32 G peak boost, 22 ft/s descent, 41.9 s total flight; fully recovered

CFD identified nose cone pressure drag as dominant contributor for the Altitude vehicle (higher C_D than Mars Rover); validated smooth flow attachment (no separation) and quadratic drag scaling under acceleration
Coordinated CAD, simulation, and physical build across 5-person team; debugged motor igniter slippage failure at competition; methodology contributed to BNL-level research publication
